However, because it is a survey it offers no direct measurement of whether sleep/wellbeing advice is situation-driven vs person-driven; it should be cited for taxonomy and to point at the underlying empirical persona-bias papers rather than for primary data.
quotable_result: "Gupta et al. (2023) ... highlight the deep-seated stereotypical biases found in LLMs assigned with socio-demographic personas. Additionally, Zhao et al. (2024) find that applying role-play often increases the overall likelihood of generating stereotypical and harmful outputs," and "Vijjini et al. (2024) show that LLMs suffer from personalization bias when they are personalized for the user's demographic."

one_liner: A quantitative framework (OpinionQA) measuring how well 9 LMs' opinion distributions on subjective Pew survey questions align with 60 US demographic groups, finding substantial default misalignment skewed toward certain groups and only modest, incomplete steerability when prompted with a user's demographic identity.
methodology: The authors build OpinionQA from 15 Pew "American Trends Panel" polls (1498 multiple-choice questions across 23 coarse topics), and probe 9 LMs (350M-178B params; OpenAI ada/davinci/text-davinci-00{1,2,3} and AI21 j1-grande/jumbo/v2-beta) by reading next-token log-probs over answer choices to form a model opinion distribution. Alignment is the 1-Wasserstein distance between the LM distribution and a group's human response distribution. They study three axes: representativeness (default opinion vs. overall populace or a group), steerability (alignment when the prompt is given the group's identity via three persona-injection formats: QA, BIO, PORTRAY), and consistency (whether the best-aligned group is stable across topics). Persona/identity is supplied only as explicit context prepended to the question.
identity_cues_tested: political ideology / party affiliation (Democrat, Republican, liberal, moderate, conservative)
religion (Protestant, Catholic, Jewish, Mormon, Muslim, Hindu, Buddhist, religious attendance)
key_findings: Default LM opinion distributions are substantially misaligned with the general US public: for most models, agreement is comparable to the Democrat-Republican gap on climate change, and every one of the 60 demographic groups is individually more representative of the US populace than any LM.

RLHF/human-feedback tuning makes alignment worse and shifts the best-aligned group from moderate/conservative/low-income (base LMs) toward liberal, high-income, well-educated, non-religious people (text-* models), matching reported InstructGPT crowdworker demographics.

text-davinci-003 collapses to near-deterministic modal opinions (often >0.99 prob on one option, e.g., >99% approval of Joe Biden), embodying caricatures of liberals/moderates rather than the diverse spread of real group opinions.

Steering toward a target group (QA/BIO/PORTRAY persona prompts, 22 groups, 500 contentious questions) yields only modest, roughly constant improvement; group-alignment disparities persist and none of the representativeness gaps are resolved by steering.
Several large groups are poorly represented by ALL models regardless of conditioning: 65+, widowed, Mormon, and high religious attendance. Models rarely refuse (1-2%) even on contentious topics.

Consistency is low: the group an LM aligns with varies by topic (e.g., generally-liberal text-davinci-00{2,3} take conservative stances on religion), so models express a patchwork of opinions rather than one coherent persona.
metrics: Representativeness R^O_m / R^G_m (mean 1-Wasserstein-based alignment, 0-1)
Steerability S^G_m (best-over-QA/BIO/PORTRAY alignment to target group)
Consistency C_m (fraction of topics where best-aligned group matches overall best group)
Refusal rate
1-Wasserstein distance over ordinal answer choices
datasets: OpinionQA (authors' new dataset, 1498 questions from 15 Pew American Trends Panel polls)
Pew Research American Trends Panel (ATP) surveys with ~10,000 US panelists, 60 demographic groups
relevance_to_hypothesis: Directly supports the person-driven side of the hypothesis: it demonstrates that an LM's expressed stance on subjective/value-laden questions is NOT uniform but skews toward specific user/demographic profiles, and that explicitly telling the model who the user is (persona injection via QA/BIO/PORTRAY) shifts its responses toward that group's views. However, the effect is only moderate and bounded: steering produces modest, incomplete adaptation, default opinions are dominated by a fixed skew (toward liberal/educated/wealthy for RLHF models), and some groups (65+, widowed) can't be reached even when conditioned on. The study covers subjective opinion/advice content but uses forced multiple-choice survey items rather than open-ended advice like "go to sleep," and notably does NOT manipulate the situation/circumstance independently of person identity, so it speaks to person-conditioning of opinions but not to whether wellbeing advice is situation-driven. It is a strong methodological template (vary identity cue, measure distributional shift) for testing whether sleep/wellbeing advice is person-conditioned.
quotable_result: "on most topics, LM opinions agree with that of the US populace about as much as Democrats and Republicans on climate change"; and RLHF models like text-davinci-003 assign >0.99 probability to a single option (e.g., >99% approval of Joe Biden), while steering toward 22 demographic groups improves but never closes group-alignment gaps.
